hawkish_phrases: inflation had continued to be higher than projected and had increased both in December and in January, when it had reached 5.1%
risks to the inflation outlook were tilted to the upside, particularly in the near term
the longer the period of elevated inflation lasted, the higher the risk of material second-round effects would become
Some members argued that the likelihood that inflation would settle at around 2% over the medium term had already increased significantly
the main risk was no longer of tightening monetary policy too early but too late
dovish_phrases: monetary support was still needed for the economy to advance further and for inflation to stabilise durably at the 2% target
there were no incipient signs of sustained domestically generated inflation
there were no signs of an upside unanchoring of inflation expectations in the euro area
the Governing Council should avoid an overreaction to inflation developments that were assessed as being short-lived
inflation was expected to decline in the course of the year
summary: The Governing Council held rates unchanged with unanimous agreement to maintain the December decisions (PEPP net purchases ending in March, gradual APP reduction), but adjusted communication to acknowledge inflation remaining elevated longer than expected, increased optionality, and upside risks, while emphasizing the need to await March staff projections before judging whether forward guidance criteria are met.
